"use client";

import { useState } from "react";

import { Card, CardContent, CardHeader, CardTitle } from "@/components/ui/card";
import { DEMO_PIN_REGISTRY, type PinSnapshot } from "@/lib/pin_replay_demo";
import { detectRegressions } from "@/lib/pin_regression";

import { DiffSidePanel } from "./components/diff_side_panel";
import { PinTimeseries } from "./components/pin_timeseries";

const PIN_DATES = Object.keys(DEMO_PIN_REGISTRY).sort();

function SnapshotCard({
  testid,
  label,
  selected_date,
  on_change,
  snapshot,
}: {
  testid: string;
  label: string;
  selected_date: string;
  on_change: (next: string) => void;
  snapshot: PinSnapshot;
}) {
  return (
    <Card data-testid={testid}>
      <CardHeader className="flex flex-row items-center justify-between gap-3">
        <CardTitle className="text-base">{label}</CardTitle>
        <select
          data-testid={`${testid}-date`}
          value={selected_date}
          onChange={(e) => on_change(e.target.value)}
          className="border-border bg-background rounded border px-2 py-1 text-sm"
        >
          {PIN_DATES.map((d) => (
            <option key={d} value={d}>
              {d}
            </option>
          ))}
        </select>
      </CardHeader>
      <CardContent className="text-sm">
        <dl className="grid grid-cols-2 gap-x-4 gap-y-1">
          <dt className="text-muted-foreground">Query</dt>
          <dd
            data-testid={`${testid}-query`}
            className="text-foreground truncate"
          >
            {snapshot.query}
          </dd>
          <dt className="text-muted-foreground">Verdict</dt>
          <dd data-testid={`${testid}-verdict`} className="text-foreground">
            {snapshot.verdict}
          </dd>
          <dt className="text-muted-foreground">Sections kept / dropped</dt>
          <dd data-testid={`${testid}-sections`} className="text-foreground">
            {snapshot.section_count_kept} / {snapshot.section_count_dropped}
          </dd>
          <dt className="text-muted-foreground">Verified sentences</dt>
          <dd data-testid={`${testid}-sentences`} className="text-foreground">
            {snapshot.verified_sentence_count}
          </dd>
          <dt className="text-muted-foreground">Pass rate</dt>
          <dd data-testid={`${testid}-pass-rate`} className="text-foreground">
            {Math.round(snapshot.pass_rate * 100)}%
          </dd>
        </dl>
      </CardContent>
    </Card>
  );
}

function EmptyPinReplay() {
  // I-cd-029 (#619): /pin_replay rebuild — empty-state copy. G1+G6 fix:
  // <main> dropped (AppShell provides it). G2 fix: removed Issue id
  // breadcrumbs from user-visible copy.
  return (
    <section
      data-testid="pin-replay-empty"
      className="mx-auto max-w-5xl px-6 py-8"
    >
      <h1 className="text-2xl font-semibold tracking-tight">
        Pin replay — same query on different dates
      </h1>
      <p className="text-muted-foreground mt-4 text-sm">
        No pin data available yet. Open a completed research run and the
        timeseries of its pin snapshots will render here.
      </p>
    </section>
  );
}

export default function PinReplayPage() {
  // I-cd-017 (#627): DEMO_PIN_REGISTRY removed — page falls back to empty
  // state until Seq 29 / I-A-12 / #619 wires the live route client. The
  // guard runs BEFORE detectRegressions / delta math (Codex iter-2 P2.2)
  // so undefined snapshot dereferences cannot crash the empty-state path.
  const [date_a, set_date_a] = useState(PIN_DATES[0] ?? "");
  const [date_b, set_date_b] = useState(
    PIN_DATES.includes("2026-04-30") ? "2026-04-30" : (PIN_DATES[0] ?? ""),
  );
  const [diff_open, set_diff_open] = useState(false);

  if (PIN_DATES.length === 0) {
    return <EmptyPinReplay />;
  }

  const snap_a = DEMO_PIN_REGISTRY[date_a];
  const snap_b = DEMO_PIN_REGISTRY[date_b];
  const all_snapshots = Object.values(DEMO_PIN_REGISTRY);
  const alerts = detectRegressions(snap_a, snap_b);

  const delta_pass_rate =
    Math.round(snap_b.pass_rate * 100) - Math.round(snap_a.pass_rate * 100);
  const delta_sentences =
    snap_b.verified_sentence_count - snap_a.verified_sentence_count;

  // I-cd-029 (#619): /pin_replay populated-state. G1+G6 fix: <main>
  // dropped (AppShell provides it). G2 fix: removed "M-INT-0b
  // post-Carney" dev-language marker; subtitle is now neutral.
  return (
    <section
      data-testid="pin-replay-page"
      className="mx-auto max-w-5xl px-6 py-8"
    >
      <div>
        <h1 className="text-2xl font-semibold tracking-tight">
          Pin replay — same query on different dates
        </h1>
        <p className="text-muted-foreground mt-2 text-sm">
          Compare two completed run snapshots side-by-side; regressions in
          verified-sentence count or pass-rate raise an alert.
        </p>
        {alerts.length > 0 ? (
          <div
            data-testid="regression-alert"
            role="alert"
            className="mt-4 rounded border border-rose-500/40 bg-rose-500/5 p-3 text-sm text-rose-700 dark:text-rose-300"
          >
            <strong className="block">⚠ Regression detected</strong>
            <ul className="mt-1 space-y-1 text-xs">
              {alerts.map((alert) => (
                <li
                  key={alert.metric}
                  data-testid={`regression-alert-${alert.metric}`}
                >
                  {alert.metric}: dropped {alert.drop}
                  {alert.unit === "pct" ? " pct points" : " sentences"} ( A=
                  {alert.a_value}
                  {alert.unit === "pct" ? "%" : ""}, B=
                  {alert.b_value}
                  {alert.unit === "pct" ? "%" : ""}, threshold=
                  {alert.threshold}
                  {alert.unit === "pct" ? " pct" : ""})
                  {alert.attributed_to_retraction &&
                  alert.attributed_to_retraction.length > 0 ? (
                    <span
                      data-testid="regression-retraction-attribution"
                      className="text-muted-foreground ml-1"
                    >
                      (attributed to retraction of:{" "}
                      {alert.attributed_to_retraction.join(", ")})
                    </span>
                  ) : null}
                </li>
              ))}
            </ul>
          </div>
        ) : null}
        <div className="mt-6 grid grid-cols-1 gap-4 md:grid-cols-2">
          <SnapshotCard
            testid="pin-snapshot-a"
            label="Snapshot A"
            selected_date={date_a}
            on_change={set_date_a}
            snapshot={snap_a}
          />
          <SnapshotCard
            testid="pin-snapshot-b"
            label="Snapshot B"
            selected_date={date_b}
            on_change={set_date_b}
            snapshot={snap_b}
          />
        </div>
        <div className="mt-6">
          <PinTimeseries snapshots={all_snapshots} />
        </div>
        <button
          type="button"
          data-testid="pin-show-diff"
          onClick={() => set_diff_open(true)}
          className="border-border mt-4 rounded border px-4 py-2 text-sm hover:bg-blue-500/10"
        >
          Show snapshot diff
        </button>
        <DiffSidePanel
          open={diff_open}
          onOpenChange={set_diff_open}
          snapshot_a={snap_a}
          snapshot_b={snap_b}
        />
        <Card data-testid="pin-replay-delta" className="mt-4">
          <CardHeader>
            <CardTitle className="text-base">Delta (B − A)</CardTitle>
          </CardHeader>
          <CardContent className="text-sm">
            <p>
              Pass rate:{" "}
              <span data-testid="pin-replay-delta-pass-rate">
                {delta_pass_rate >= 0 ? "+" : ""}
                {delta_pass_rate}%
              </span>
            </p>
            <p>
              Verified sentences:{" "}
              <span data-testid="pin-replay-delta-sentences">
                {delta_sentences >= 0 ? "+" : ""}
                {delta_sentences}
              </span>
            </p>
          </CardContent>
        </Card>
      </div>
    </section>
  );
}

defaults:
  run:
    working-directory: web

jobs:
  lint_format_typecheck_build:
    name: lint + format + typecheck + build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: setup_node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: "npm"
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json

      - name: install_dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: format_check
        run: npm run format:check

      - name: typecheck
        run: npm run typecheck

      - name: build
        run: npm run build

  e2e_playwright:
    name: e2e + a11y + perf (Playwright on Chromium)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: lint_format_typecheck_build
    steps:
      - name: checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: setup_node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: "npm"
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json

      - name: setup_python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.13"
          cache: "pip"

      - name: install_python_dependencies
        working-directory: ${{ github.workspace }}
        run: |
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: install_npm_dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: install_playwright_chromium
        run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium

      - name: start_fastapi_backend
        working-directory: ${{ github.workspace }}
        run: |
          PYTHONPATH=src nohup python -m uvicorn polaris_v6.api.app:app \
            --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000 \
            > /tmp/fastapi.log 2>&1 &
          # Wait for /health to respond.
          for i in {1..30}; do
            if curl -fs http://127.0.0.1:8000/health > /dev/null; then
              echo "backend ready"
              exit 0
            fi
            sleep 1
          done
          echo "backend failed to start"
          cat /tmp/fastapi.log
          exit 1

      - name: build_and_start_next
        # I-cd-015 (GH#611): POLARIS_TEST_HARNESS_ENABLED=1 lets the prod
        # build serve harness routes for tests that depend on them
        # (evidence_tooltip_perf etc.). Real production leaves this UNSET
        # and the middleware 404s /charts_test/*, /sentence_hover_test/*,
        # /disambiguation_modal_preview/*, /generation/*, /retrieval/*, /sse/*.
        env:
          POLARIS_TEST_HARNESS_ENABLED: "1"
        run: |
          npm run build
          nohup npx next start -p 3738 > /tmp/next.log 2>&1 &
          # Wait for /dashboard to respond.
          for i in {1..30}; do
            if curl -fs http://127.0.0.1:3738/dashboard > /dev/null; then
              echo "frontend ready"
              exit 0
            fi
            sleep 1
          done
          echo "frontend failed to start"
          cat /tmp/next.log
          exit 1

      - name: run_e2e_inspector
        env:
          SCREENSHOT_BASE_URL: http://127.0.0.1:3738
          POLARIS_V6_BACKEND_URL: http://127.0.0.1:8000

// I-cd-029 (#619): /pin_replay route G1-G8 acceptance gates.

import { expect, test } from "@playwright/test";

const BANNED_DEV_LANGUAGE = [
  /\bslice\b/i,
  /\bscaffold\b/i,
  /\bplaceholder\b/i,
  /\bphase 0\b/i,
  /\bphase 1\b/i,
  /\bphase 2[a-z]?\b/i,
  /\bF4 plan\b/i,
  /\bpost[- ]carney\b/i,
  /\bi-cd-/i,
  /\bM-INT-0b\b/i,
  /\bI-A-12\b/i,
];

test("G1 + G6: /pin_replay has exactly one header + one main", async ({
  page,
}) => {
  await page.goto("/pin_replay");
  await expect(page.locator("header")).toHaveCount(1);
  await expect(page.locator("main")).toHaveCount(1);
});

test("G2: /pin_replay contains no banned dev-language strings (body + titles + aria-labels)", async ({
  page,
}) => {
  await page.goto("/pin_replay");
  const body_text = (await page.locator("body").textContent()) || "";
  const title_text =
    (await page
      .locator("[title]")
      .evaluateAll((els: Element[]) =>
        els.map((el) => el.getAttribute("title") || "").join(" · "),
      )) || "";
  const aria_text =
    (await page
      .locator("[aria-label]")
      .evaluateAll((els: Element[]) =>
        els.map((el) => el.getAttribute("aria-label") || "").join(" · "),
      )) || "";
  const all_text = `${body_text} · ${title_text} · ${aria_text}`;
  for (const banned of BANNED_DEV_LANGUAGE) {
    expect(all_text).not.toMatch(banned);
  }
});

test("G1 nav parity: primary nav visible on /pin_replay", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/pin_replay");
  const nav = page.locator("nav[aria-label='Primary']");
  await expect(nav).toBeVisible();
  for (const label of [
    "Home",
    "Intake",
    "Dashboard",
    "Upload",
    "Benchmark",
    "Contracts",
    "Pin Replay",
    "Memory",
  ]) {
    await expect(nav.getByRole("link", { name: label })).toBeVisible();
  }
});

test("G8: /pin_replay renders with zero console errors", async ({ page }) => {
  const errors: string[] = [];
  page.on("console", (msg) => {
    if (msg.type() === "error") errors.push(msg.text());
  });
  await page.goto("/pin_replay");
  await page.waitForLoadState("domcontentloaded");
  await page.waitForTimeout(1500);
  expect(errors).toEqual([]);
});

 succeeded in 1120ms:
/**
 * I-f13-003: Inline regression alerts on the pin-replay page. Hard-coded
 * thresholds; production reads from a regression-config file (post-Carney).
 *
 * I-f13-004: source-retraction context attribution for sentence-count drops.
 */

import type { PinSnapshot } from "@/lib/pin_replay_demo";

export const REGRESSION_THRESHOLDS = {
  pass_rate_pct_drop: 5,
  verified_sentence_count_drop: 3,
} as const;

export interface RegressionAlert {
  metric: "pass_rate" | "verified_sentence_count";
  a_value: number;
  b_value: number;
  drop: number;
  threshold: number;
  unit: "pct" | "count";
  attributed_to_retraction?: string[];
}

export interface RetractionContext {
  newly_retracted: string[];
}

export function getRetractionContext(
  a: PinSnapshot,
  b: PinSnapshot,
): RetractionContext {
  const a_ids = a.retracted_source_ids ?? [];
  const newly_retracted = (b.retracted_source_ids ?? []).filter(
    (id) => !a_ids.includes(id),
  );
  return { newly_retracted };
}

export function detectRegressions(
  a: PinSnapshot,
  b: PinSnapshot,
): RegressionAlert[] {
  const alerts: RegressionAlert[] = [];
  const retraction = getRetractionContext(a, b);

  const a_pass_pct = Math.round(a.pass_rate * 100);
  const b_pass_pct = Math.round(b.pass_rate * 100);
  const pass_drop = a_pass_pct - b_pass_pct;
  if (pass_drop > REGRESSION_THRESHOLDS.pass_rate_pct_drop) {
    alerts.push({
      metric: "pass_rate",
      a_value: a_pass_pct,
      b_value: b_pass_pct,
      drop: pass_drop,
      threshold: REGRESSION_THRESHOLDS.pass_rate_pct_drop,
      unit: "pct",
    });
  }

  const sentence_drop = a.verified_sentence_count - b.verified_sentence_count;
  if (sentence_drop > REGRESSION_THRESHOLDS.verified_sentence_count_drop) {
    const alert: RegressionAlert = {
      metric: "verified_sentence_count",
      a_value: a.verified_sentence_count,
      b_value: b.verified_sentence_count,
      drop: sentence_drop,
      threshold: REGRESSION_THRESHOLDS.verified_sentence_count_drop,
      unit: "count",
    };
    if (retraction.newly_retracted.length > 0) {
      alert.attributed_to_retraction = retraction.newly_retracted;
    }
    alerts.push(alert);
  }

  return alerts;
}

 succeeded in 1359ms:
// I-cd-022 (#612): pathname-aware AppShell gate. On `/`, suppress BOTH
// the AppShell header AND its <main> wrapper so the home route can
// provide its own header (with the same primary nav) + its own <main>.
// This avoids G1 (double header) + G6 (nested main) landmark violations.
//
// Tiny "use client" boundary so the otherwise server-rendered AppShell
// can still wrap every non-home route on the server.
"use client";

import { usePathname } from "next/navigation";

import { AppShell } from "@/components/app_shell";

interface AppShellGateProps {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}

export function AppShellGate({ children }: AppShellGateProps) {
  const pathname = usePathname();
  // Home owns its own header + main; render children bare on `/`.
  if (pathname === "/") {
    return <>{children}</>;
  }
  return <AppShell>{children}</AppShell>;
}

import Link from "next/link";

import { NavLink } from "@/components/nav_link";

/**
 * I-cd-004: the global app shell. A server component that wraps every prod
 * route with a single header + primary nav. The 8 primary nav entries are
 * the Codex-APPROVED prod route map (see docs/web/route_map.md):
 *   Home · Intake · Dashboard · Upload · Benchmark · Contracts · Pin Replay · Memory
 *
 * Out of scope for this issue (each its own later issue per the Seq-N order):
 *  - /sign-in: handled by I-cd-014's auth rebuild; not surfaced in the nav.
 *  - /inspector/[runId] + /runs/[runId]: run-scoped, deep-linked from
 *    /dashboard rather than top-nav entries.
 *  - /audit_live: ABSORBED into /runs/[runId] at I-cd-025; standalone route
 *    retired at that point (not merely hidden — per Codex P2).
 *  - /generation, /retrieval, /sse: CUT-from-prod; prod-build exclusion is
 *    I-cd-015.
 *  - Auth gating of the nav itself is deferred to I-cd-014.
 *  - Per-page rebuilds happen in the I-cd-013..030 series.
 */
const PRIMARY_NAV: ReadonlyArray<{ href: string; label: string }> = [
  { href: "/", label: "Home" },
  { href: "/intake", label: "Intake" },
  { href: "/dashboard", label: "Dashboard" },
  { href: "/upload", label: "Upload" },
  { href: "/benchmark", label: "Benchmark" },
  { href: "/contracts", label: "Contracts" },
  { href: "/pin_replay", label: "Pin Replay" },
  { href: "/memory", label: "Memory" },
];

export function AppShell({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <>
      <header className="border-border bg-background sticky top-0 z-40 border-b">
        <div className="mx-auto flex max-w-7xl items-center gap-6 px-6 py-3">
          <Link
            href="/"
            className="text-foreground font-mono text-sm font-semibold tracking-tight"
          >
            POLARIS · Canada
          </Link>
          <nav className="flex items-center gap-1" aria-label="Primary">
            {PRIMARY_NAV.map((item) => (
              <NavLink key={item.href} href={item.href}>
                {item.label}
              </NavLink>
            ))}
          </nav>
        </div>
      </header>
      <main className="flex-1">{children}</main>
    </>
  );
}

// I-cd-027 (#617): /benchmark route G1-G8 acceptance gates.

import { expect, test } from "@playwright/test";

const BANNED_DEV_LANGUAGE = [
  /\bslice\b/i,
  /\bscaffold\b/i,
  /\bplaceholder\b/i,
  /\bphase 0\b/i,
  /\bphase 1\b/i,
  /\bphase 2[a-z]?\b/i,
  /\bF4 plan\b/i,
  /\bpost[- ]carney\b/i,
  /\bi-cd-/i,
];

test("G1 + G6: /benchmark has exactly one header + one main", async ({
  page,
}) => {
  await page.goto("/benchmark");
  await expect(page.locator("header")).toHaveCount(1);
  await expect(page.locator("main")).toHaveCount(1);
});

test("G2: /benchmark contains no banned dev-language strings (body + titles + aria-labels)", async ({
  page,
}) => {
  await page.goto("/benchmark");
  const body_text = (await page.locator("body").textContent()) || "";
  const title_text =
    (await page
      .locator("[title]")
      .evaluateAll((els: Element[]) =>
        els.map((el) => el.getAttribute("title") || "").join(" · "),
      )) || "";
  const aria_text =
    (await page
      .locator("[aria-label]")
      .evaluateAll((els: Element[]) =>
        els.map((el) => el.getAttribute("aria-label") || "").join(" · "),
      )) || "";
  const all_text = `${body_text} · ${title_text} · ${aria_text}`;
  for (const banned of BANNED_DEV_LANGUAGE) {
    expect(all_text).not.toMatch(banned);
  }
});

test("G1 nav parity: primary nav visible on /benchmark", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/benchmark");
  const nav = page.locator("nav[aria-label='Primary']");
  await expect(nav).toBeVisible();
  for (const label of [
    "Home",
    "Intake",
    "Dashboard",
    "Upload",
    "Benchmark",
    "Contracts",
    "Pin Replay",
    "Memory",
  ]) {
    await expect(nav.getByRole("link", { name: label })).toBeVisible();
  }
});

test("G8: /benchmark renders with zero console errors", async ({ page }) => {
  const errors: string[] = [];
  page.on("console", (msg) => {
    if (msg.type() === "error") errors.push(msg.text());
  });
  await page.goto("/benchmark");
  await page.waitForLoadState("domcontentloaded");
  await page.waitForTimeout(1500);
  expect(errors).toEqual([]);
});

        <p>
          <strong className="text-foreground">Reproducible.</strong> Each
          scoreboard is produced by{" "}
          <code className="bg-muted rounded px-1 py-0.5 text-xs">
            scripts/run_benchmark.py
          </code>
          . The scoreboard.json artifact is canonical (sort_keys=True), so two
          runs against the same inputs produce identical scores. Carney&rsquo;s
          office can re-run the benchmark and compare bytewise.
        </p>
      </div>
    </section>
  );
}

state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:11:For Canadian federal AI workloads in 2026, the central trade-off is not simply \\\\u201ccheap sovereign compute\\\\u201d versus \\\\u201cexpensive cloud.\\\\u201d The real trade-off is **high-fixed-cost sovereignty with lower marginal cost** versus **high-variable-cost hyperscale access with better frontier quality, faster deployment, and richer managed controls**. Canada\\\\u2019s sovereign-compute push is now concrete: the \\\\ue200entity\\\\ue202[\\\\\"organization\\\\\",\\\\\"Government of Canada\\\\\",\\\\\"federal government of Canada\\\\\"]\\\\ue201 has committed up to C$700 million for private-sector compute capacity, up to C$1 billion for public supercomputing, and up to C$300 million for an AI Compute Access Fund; the AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program alone will provide about C$890 million to the build layer beginning in fiscal 2026\\\\u201327. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn31view0\\\\ue202turn31view1\\\\ue202turn31view2\\\\ue201\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:32:| \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Google Cloud\\\\ue202https://cloud.google.com\\\\ue201 | Official pricing snippets retrieved show **A3 Mega H100 at about US$93.40/h** on-demand, or **US$11.68/GPU-hour**; Dynamic Workload Scheduler calendar-mode pricing was shown at **US$44/h**, or **US$5.50/GPU-hour**. | Excellent accelerator and cluster software stack overall: H100, H200, B200, strong AI Hypercomputer networking. But Canadian frontier availability is narrower: Montr\\\\u00e9al and Toronto mostly expose G2/L4, while Toronto shows a limited **A3 Edge H100** serving-oriented option. | **Medium to high.** Canada data boundary helps, but the U.S. parent-company nexus remains. | Protected B authorization, Assured Workloads Canada boundary, Canadian-screened support for that package, ISO/SOC, Confidential VMs with H100, and Cloud HSM / external keys are strong differentiators. | \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn16search4\\\\ue202turn16search5\\\\ue202turn14view0\\\\ue202turn14view1\\\\ue202turn15search0\\\\ue202turn15search1\\\\ue202turn23search2\\\\ue202turn24search18\\\\ue202turn27search2\\\\ue202turn27search14\\\\ue202turn27search17\\\\ue202turn27search8\\\\ue201 |\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:42:    title \\\\\"Modeled effective cost per H100-equivalent GPU-hour\\\\\"\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:52:The other decisive variable is **cluster size**. Small sovereign enclaves are expensive even if their silicon is good, because secure operations do not scale down linearly. In the same model at 80% utilization and 5-year amortization, a 64-GPU enclave lands near **US$9.7/GPU-hour**, a 128-GPU enclave near **US$5.8**, a 256-GPU cluster near **US$4.4**, a 512-GPU cluster near **US$3.4**, and a 1,024-GPU cluster near **US$2.8**. That is why the best sovereign design for federal use is usually **one or a few pooled national cores** rather than many boutique installations. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn31view1\\\\ue202turn31view2\\\\ue202turn33view0\\\\ue201\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:56:    title \\\\\"Modeled sovereign cost by cluster size at 80% utilization\\\\\"\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:64:| Sensitivity variable | Base case | Alternative | Modeled effect | Why it matters |\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:81:Canada-region availability is where the quality gap becomes practical rather than theoretical. AWS documentation retrieved here explicitly lists P5/P5e classes in Canadian regions. Google Cloud\\\\u2019s GPU location pages show that Montr\\\\u00e9al and Toronto expose mostly G2/L4, while Toronto has a **limited A3 Edge H100** zone rather than broad A3 Mega/A3 Ultra training coverage. Azure\\\\u2019s retrieved official docs clearly document frontier GPU families overall, but Canada-region public availability was less transparent than on AWS; indexed pricing mirrors suggest Canada Central H100 exists, but the official region-availability material retrieved did not establish broad public access with the same clarity. The policy implication is straightforward: **if the workload absolutely requires the newest silicon in Canada today, public cloud options are uneven, and sovereign capacity may still require bespoke leasing or waiting lists.** \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn3search9\\\\ue202turn14view0\\\\ue202turn14view1\\\\ue202turn15search0\\\\ue202turn15search1\\\\ue202turn18search3\\\\ue202turn12search3\\\\ue201\\
state\compare_chatgpt_q1.md:145:Open questions remain. Exact negotiated federal discounts under existing cloud supply contracts are not public. Cloud GPU availability changes quickly, especially for Blackwell-era systems and Canadian regions. And support-personnel residency, key-custody terms, and audit rights vary materially by service, contract, and workload package. Any procurement decision should therefore revalidate four items at the RFP stage: **actual reserved pricing, Canada-region hardware availability, support-personnel geography and screening, and the legal posture of provider-admin access to plaintext or decrypted data**. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn42search11\\\\ue202turn23search2\\\\ue202turn27search2\\\\ue202turn25search2\\\\ue201\"]}, docs\m26_threat_model.md:28:- `UPDATE` that violates state-machine transitions, separation of duties, all-clauses-approved invariant, content immutability, attribution drift, or terminal-row immutability
docs\m26_threat_model.md:60:| `UPDATE OF status` | `trg_validate_status_transition` | Closed transition table: only `(draft → awaiting_approval)`, `(awaiting_approval → approved)`, `(awaiting_approval → rejected)`. Plus all-clauses-approved + non-empty-clauses invariants + separation-of-duties. |
docs\m26_threat_model.md:64:| Per-row | `CHECK` constraint | Status-vs-decision-fields invariants: draft/awaiting_approval → all decision fields NULL; approved → approved_by + rationale + decided_at NOT NULL & non-empty, rejected_by NULL; symmetric for rejected. Mutual exclusion between approved_by and rejected_by. |
docs\m26_threat_model.md:74:| `UPDATE` (any column) | `trg_clause_content_immutable` | title/body/evidence_ids/claim_ids are immutable from INSERT-time onward |
docs\m26_threat_model.md:78:| Per-row | `CHECK` constraint | Decision-vs-audit-fields invariants: pending → decided_by/notes/decided_at all NULL; approved → decided_by + decided_at NOT NULL & non-empty; rejected → adds decision_notes NOT NULL & non-empty |
docs\m26_threat_model.md:93:**Why not a stronger trigger on fresh-row INSERT?** Schema triggers cannot rely on caller-supplied connection state (PRAGMAs, session vars) or caller identity — the attacker controls those on their own connection. Triggers must enforce invariants from durable table state and row content (the NEW row's columns + queries against existing rows in the same database). The `trg_decision_log_no_replace` design works because the invariant ("no overwrite of an existing log_id") is checkable from `NEW.log_id` plus a query against existing log rows — both of which the schema can see deterministically. Distinguishing "legitimate trigger-driven append" from "forged direct INSERT" would require connection authentication or a session-tagged column, which SQLite/Python's per-statement authorizer hooks support but only at the application layer (and would need the application to enforce it on every connection — out of scope for this schema).

docs\opentelemetry_genai.md:30:### 2.1 Environment variable (POLARIS deployment)
state\compare_gemini_q4.md:166:Conversely, as the Bank of Canada initiated its historic rate-hiking cycle in 2022 to combat inflation, systemic risks amplified. Borrowers holding variable-rate mortgages with fixed payments faced severe negative amortization, while fixed-rate borrowers faced massive payment shocks upon renewal. In response, OSFI pivoted aggressively toward constraint mechanisms. In 2025, the regulator implemented a strict, portfolio-based Loan-to-Income (LTI) limit. This directive explicitly restricted the proportion of newly originated uninsured mortgages that federally regulated financial institutions could issue exceeding a 4.5x loan-to-income multiple. The objective was clear: to proactively guard against the dangerous buildup of highly leveraged borrowers within lender portfolios, particularly during eventual low-interest-rate periods, thereby reducing overall systemic vulnerability.

state\compare_gemini_q3.md:129:To contextualize the projected impact of generative AI on the 2026-2030 labour market, it is necessary to examine the historical dynamics of past technological shocks. The assumption that technology always disrupts the same demographics in the same manner is empirically false. Studies analyzing occupational churn—measured by changes in the employment shares of various broad occupation groups—from 1880 to 2024 reveal shifting patterns of vulnerability. The most disruptive period in the history of the North American labour market occurred between 1940 and 1970, driven by the mass transition of workers out of agrarian farming roles and into industrial manufacturing economies. Paradoxically, despite the proliferation of computers and the internet, the 1990s and 2010s were among the least disruptive decades on record in terms of raw occupational churn.   
state\compare_gemini_q3.md:204:Even with highly effective, targeted reskilling, structural barriers in modern hiring practices frequently suppress transition success. Over the past decade, employers have engaged in rampant, unjustified "upcredentialing"—for example, demanding a bachelor's degree for secretarial and administrative roles where fewer than 20% of current practitioners actually hold one to perform the tasks effectively. This artificial barrier automatically disqualifies massive segments of the labour force from participating in the economy.   
state\compare_gemini_q2.md:60:As the July 1, 2026, deadline for the first mandatory joint review of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) approaches, the fundamental architecture of North American economic integration is undergoing a severe and unprecedented stress test. What was originally designed by its architects as a routine mechanism to evaluate the performance of a modernized trade pact has rapidly evolved into a high-stakes, adversarial geoeconomic negotiation. The macroeconomic and geopolitical landscape of the spring of 2026 bears little resemblance to the environment in which CUSMA was originally negotiated, ratified, and implemented. Under the second administration of U.S. President Donald Trump, the traditional orthodoxies of free trade, comparative advantage, and open international markets have been systematically excised from the American trade policy lexicon. In their place, Washington has entrenched doctrines of perimeter defense, supply chain weaponization, and an aggressive, zero-sum mercantilism that views trading partners primarily through the lens of strategic competition and national security.   
state\compare_gemini_q2.md:78:The second pathway is the Rolling Review Purgatory. If a consensus cannot be reached during the July 2026 meetings—a scenario viewed as highly probable by trade analysts given the current adversarial posture of the Office of the United States Trade Representative (USTR)—the agreement does not immediately dissolve. Instead, CUSMA remains legally in effect but enters a period of precarious, mandated annual reviews. During this phase, the Free Trade Commission is compelled to meet every year to attempt to resolve the outstanding trilateral disputes. If no resolution is achieved over the subsequent decade of rolling reviews, the agreement simply expires on its original sunset date in 2036.   
state\compare_gemini_q2.md:82:The weaponization of this sunset clause and the threat of annual reviews by the Trump administration introduces profound systemic risks to the Canadian economy. As explicitly modeled and warned by the Bank of Canada, the outcome of the 2026 review is currently the overriding variable in the nation's economic outlook. The mere prospect of CUSMA falling into a rolling annual review cycle is highly damaging, as it effectively acts as a deterrent to foreign direct investment and long-term capital expenditure. Cross-border capital allocation relies heavily on predictability; multinational corporations are highly averse to deploying billions of dollars into capital-intensive manufacturing or resource extraction jurisdictions where the fundamental rules of origin, tariff rates, and market access privileges could be upended within a 12-month window.   
state\compare_gemini_q2.md:162:A critical, highly volatile variable in Canada's preparation is the strategic behavior of Mexico. The U.S. preference for bilateral, divide-and-conquer negotiation tactics presents a severe, existential risk to Canada's negotiating position. Throughout the early months of 2026, USTR Greer openly telegraphed to Congress and the media that bilateral negotiations with Mexico were progressing far faster and more smoothly than those with Ottawa. Greer explicitly praised the administration of Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum for independently raising tariffs on Chinese and Vietnamese imports to align with U.S. containment goals. This dynamic fueled intense anxieties within Canadian policy circles that Canada could be intentionally marginalized, sidelined, or forced to accept a pre-negotiated U.S.-Mexico framework as an unalterable fait accompli.   
state\compare_gemini_q1.md:7:I've completed your research. Feel free to ask me follow-up questions or request changes.Canada AI Compute: Sovereign vs. HyperscalerThe 2026 AI Compute Trilemma: Navigating Cost, Quality, and Jurisdiction in Canada's Federal AI WorkloadsIntroduction: The Sovereign Compute Imperative in 2026As artificial intelligence transitions from a frontier experimental capability to a foundational element of public infrastructure, the Canadian federal government is confronting a profound architectural and policy trilemma. The rapid deployment of advanced large language models (LLMs) requires infrastructure that simultaneously satisfies rigorous data sovereignty requirements, delivers world-class computational performance, and remains economically viable in an era of escalating energy and hardware costs. In 2026, this dynamic is encapsulated by the strategic choice between cultivating a domestic, sovereign AI compute ecosystem—anchored by massive federal initiatives like the Artificial Intelligence Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP) and industry clusters like SCALE AI—versus leveraging the immense scale, ecosystem maturity, and localized cryptographic adaptations of United States-headquartered hyperscalers such as Microsoft Azure, Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Google Cloud Platform (GCP).The stakes in 2026 extend far beyond the mechanics of public procurement; they encompass national security, economic competitiveness, and the absolute preservation of domestic intellectual property. The Canadian government has explicitly designated digital sovereignty as a paramount objective, aiming to exercise autonomy over digital infrastructure, proprietary data, and critical technologies while aggressively insulating federal operations from extraterritorial legal frameworks. Concurrently, the global demand for AI compute has reached staggering proportions. By the end of 2025, total global AI data center power capacity exceeded 30 gigawatts—a figure comparable to the peak power usage of entire industrialized states such as New York, and substantially larger than the baseline demand of many developed nations. Within this constrained and fiercely competitive global market, Canada has launched a $2 billion Sovereign AI Compute Strategy to secure domestic capacity, committing approximately $890 million to the Infrastructure Build Layer of SCIP alone.   However, global hyperscalers have not remained static in the face of these protectionist shifts. Recognizing the vulnerability of their lucrative public sector market share to rising data sovereignty concerns, firms like Microsoft have countered with historic investments. Microsoft's unprecedented $19 billion commitment to Canada introduces localized cloud controls, confidential computing enclaves, and contractual legal shields designed to seamlessly mimic the benefits of physical sovereignty while retaining global cloud agility. Furthermore, the fundamental economic foundations of Canadian computing are undergoing a paradigm shift. Historically heavily reliant on abundant, inexpensive hydroelectric power, jurisdictions like Quebec are sharply increasing tariffs for energy-intensive AI data centers to manage grid stability and protect residential consumers. This pricing evolution fundamentally alters the unit economics of domestic model training, eroding the long-standing geographic arbitrage that previously favored Canadian deployments.   This comprehensive report exhaustively analyzes the cost-quality-jurisdiction trade-off inherent in Canada's federal AI compute strategy. By meticulously examining the legal mechanisms of extraterritorial data access under United States law, the shifting economics of frontier hardware like the NVIDIA B200 Blackwell architecture, the severe impact of provincial power rate adjustments, and the software stack maturity of competing computing ecosystems, this analysis provides a definitive framework for optimizing federal AI workloads across strict Protected B and Protected C security classifications.The Jurisdictional Imperative and the Architecture of Data SovereigntyThe foundational tension driving the federal compute procurement debate is the definition, enforcement, and technological realization of data sovereignty. For the Government of Canada, digital sovereignty is not merely a political aspiration but a strict, legally binding operational requirement, particularly governed by bodies such as Shared Services Canada (SSC) and informed by legislative updates including Bill C-26 and the Critical Cyber Systems Protection Act. The classification of federal data dictates the stringency of these sovereignty controls. Protected B encompasses information that could cause serious injury to an individual or government organization if compromised, such as detailed financial information or medical records. Protected C represents the highest tier of non-classified sensitivity, encompassing information that could cause extremely grave injury, including national security intelligence, advanced defense logistics, and highly sensitive diplomatic communications.   The Extraterritorial Reach of the US CLOUD ActThe primary catalyst for Canada's aggressive sovereign compute push is the United States Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data (CLOUD) Act of 2018. The CLOUD Act fundamentally altered the international data jurisdiction landscape by establishing that United States law enforcement authorities can compel US-based service providers to disclose data regardless of where that data is physically stored around the globe. For federal planners, this legislation laid bare the distinction between mere data residency and genuine data sovereignty.   The application of the CLOUD Act to Canadian data operates through three distinct and pervasive legal pathways. The first is direct application; when Canadian entities utilize services provided directly by US-headquartered corporations, the data is subject to US jurisdiction via the parent company's geographic domicile. The second, and most legally complex, is application through corporate control. The CLOUD Act's "possession, custody, or control" doctrine extends the legislation's reach to data held by foreign subsidiaries of US companies. This means that a Canadian-incorporated subsidiary of a global hyperscaler is still legally bound to comply with United States judicial orders, rendering local incorporation an insufficient shield. The third pathway involves application to foreign companies with a US presence; any service provider with a sufficient operational footprint in the US, regardless of its global headquarters, may be subject to the Act's sweeping provisions.   For federal departments operating under the mandate of Shared Services Canada (SSC), this sweeping extraterritoriality creates a severe and unacceptable compliance vulnerability. The SSC has explicitly stated in policy white papers that reliance on hyperscale cloud service providers (CSPs) with foreign operations means Canada simply cannot ensure full sovereignty over its data. This exposes sensitive government assets to foreign laws and potential disclosure to a foreign government, potentially without the Government of Canada's prior knowledge or consent.   This is not a hypothetical academic risk. It is the operational reality of utilizing foreign infrastructure. When a senior Microsoft executive was asked under oath before the French Senate in June 2025 whether he could guarantee that French government data stored in Microsoft's cloud would never be transmitted to US authorities without French authorization, his answer was unequivocal: "Non, je ne peux pas le garantir" ("No, I cannot guarantee it"). The identical legal reality applies to Canadian federal data. Storing data on Canadian soil does not automatically protect it; the CLOUD Act compels disclosure based on who ultimately controls the data, not strictly where the server racks are physically bolted down.   While data residency ensures physical storage within Canada, the US CLOUD Act asserts jurisdiction based on corporate control. True digital sovereignty requires the infrastructure operator to be exclusively subject to Canadian law, insulating data from extraterritorial legal demands. The architectural distinction is stark: a hyperscaler framework permits a foreign legal order to bypass physical geographic borders via corporate parent-subsidiary hierarchies, whereas a fully sovereign architecture halts such jurisdictional overreach directly at the national border due to independent, air-gapped domestic ownership. Encryption at rest, while an industry-standard security baseline, does not mitigate this specific jurisdictional risk if the cloud provider retains the ability to access the decryption keys or view the decrypted data during active computational processing.   The Hyperscaler Response: Cryptographic Sovereignty and Market DefenseRecognizing the existential threat that strict data sovereignty mandates pose to their massive public sector market share, United States hyperscalers have heavily criticized policies that conflate geographic server location with genuine security and resilience. Prominent think tanks and industry analysts argue that the risk of foreign legal orders is a theoretical extremity that ignores the practical, empirical reality of modern cloud security. Public transparency reports published by AWS, Microsoft, and Google indicate zero instances of Canadian enterprise or government content being disclosed to US law enforcement under the CLOUD Act, despite tens of thousands of routine global law enforcement demands.   To address the jurisdictional gap without fracturing their highly integrated global network architectures, hyperscalers have pioneered a concept that can be termed "cryptographic sovereignty." In 2026, Microsoft launched its Sovereign AI Landing Zone (SAIL) in Canada, backed by an unprecedented $19 billion capital investment pledge. This advanced architecture utilizes Azure Confidential Compute, a technology that keeps data strictly encrypted and isolated at the hardware level even while it is actively being processed in memory by AI models. Coupled with external key management via Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) that remain entirely under the Canadian client's control, hyperscalers assert that they are mathematically and physically incapable of complying with a foreign subpoena because they lack the cryptographic means to access the plaintext data, thereby neutralizing the CLOUD Act's reach.   Furthermore, hyperscalers have instituted rigorous organizational and contractual controls tailored specifically for the Canadian federal government. Google Cloud's Data Boundary for the Government of Canada ensures that technical support for Protected B workloads is provided exclusively by Canadian personnel who have completed extensive Reliability Status security screenings. The supported cloud services have successfully completed the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS) rigorous assessment to support Protected B Medium (PBMM) and Protected B High Value Asset (PBHVA) workloads.   Amazon Web Services (AWS) offers similar verifiable controls over data access, combined with strict, codified contractual commitments to legally challenge any inappropriate or overbroad foreign data requests. AWS emphasizes that the CLOUD Act does not prohibit technical measures and operational controls designed to prevent access to customer data, asserting that their technical controls create multiple layers of protection that operate independently of corporate jurisdiction.   Despite these highly advanced technical mitigations, cryptographic sovereignty remains a heavily debated substitute for absolute legal sovereignty. As long as the physical infrastructure and underlying hypervisor are operated by a United States entity, theoretical attack vectors—or unprecedented extraterritorial legal maneuvers compelling the alteration of the host operating environment itself—remain a nonzero risk. For the most critical Protected C AI workloads, where the risk tolerance is functionally zero, this dependency renders the hyperscaler approach highly contentious.   The Domestic Ecosystem: SCALE AI and the Sovereign Compute StrategyTo establish a definitive, legally impregnable alternative to foreign CSPs, the Government of Canada initiated the $2 billion Canadian Sovereign AI Compute Strategy. This strategy is an aggressive, interventionist industrial policy designed to rectify the systemic underinvestment in domestic infrastructure that previously forced promising Canadian AI startups to incorporate abroad or rely entirely on United States infrastructure to achieve scale.   The AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP)The centerpiece of this federal strategy is the AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP), a highly structured initiative designed to construct a massive, publicly accessible AI supercomputer. Backed by approximately $890 million directed explicitly toward the Infrastructure Build Layer, SCIP represents a coordinated national effort to repatriate compute capacity and anchor a secure ecosystem.   A critical element of SCIP is its uncompromising sovereignty criteria, explicitly drafted to counter the CLOUD Act. Section 6 of the program guidelines strictly mandates that core compute and storage infrastructure must be owned or contractually controlled by Canadian entities, implementing robust safeguards to ensure no foreign party can unilaterally restrict use or demand access. The program insists that Canadian data, including highly sensitive datasets, must have appropriate protections while being leveraged to build AI solutions that directly address Canadian needs.   The SCIP architecture is deliberately bifurcated into two integrated operational layers to ensure the hardware is effectively utilized by the broader innovation ecosystem:The Infrastructure Build Layer: This component is responsible for the physical design, construction, systems administration, hardware operations, and long-term data center management.   The National Service Layer: This layer is tasked with transforming "bare metal" hardware into a highly usable platform. This includes providing the user interface, extensive APIs, AI and machine learning compute-as-a-service frameworks, active data management repositories, and seamless integration with Canada's existing Digital Research Infrastructure (DRI) ecosystem.   The Role of SCALE AI and Digital ClustersComplementing the raw infrastructure build is the deployment of specialized funding and industry integration through national clusters. Scale AI, Canada's AI-Powered Supply Chains Cluster based in Montréal, operates under a co-investment model that bridges the gap between Canada's world-class AI research and its commercial deployment. By late 2025, Scale AI announced a historic, record-breaking $128.5 million CAD funding round to support 44 new applied AI projects, driving collective investments toward the $500 million mark.   The critical sovereignty aspect of Scale AI's operations is its strict retention of intellectual property. As of March 2025, 100% of the IP created in the cluster's first 100 projects remained Canadian-owned, ensuring that the algorithmic innovations generated domestically are not immediately absorbed by foreign conglomerates. Projects funded range from automating municipal infrastructure management to complex port logistics optimization tools, demonstrating the broad utility of the ecosystem. Concurrently, the Vancouver-based Digital Global Innovation Cluster (DIGITAL) is advancing Canada's leadership in emerging digital technologies, injecting further capital into collaborative, industry-led projects.   Private Sector Alignment: TELUS and Alpha ComputeThe federal strategy is further amplified by massive private-sector investments explicitly aligning with the sovereign mandate. The telecommunications giant TELUS, in direct collaboration with the Government of Canada, has pioneered a network of Sovereign AI Factories. Following the rapid, unprecedented sell-out of its initial facility in Rimouski, Quebec, TELUS is executing a massive expansion into British Columbia with a cluster projected to scale to an astonishing 60,000 high-performance GPUs and 150 megawatts of power demand by 2032. This project represents a $9 billion economic injection and is explicitly marketed as a secure alternative to global hyperscalers. TELUS guarantees that intellectual property and sensitive data will never traverse outside Canadian borders, while running the entire operation on 98% clean hydroelectric energy and utilizing advanced liquid cooling with heat recovery technologies.   Additionally, highly agile domestic infrastructure firms like Alpha Compute have demonstrated the capability to deploy frontier hardware at speeds rivaling global tech giants. In early 2026, Alpha Compute officially activated the ALPHA-01 cluster in a Canadian datacenter, featuring 504 of the latest NVIDIA B200 GPUs, with aggressive plans to scale beyond 1,000 GPUs using specialized, hardware-backed non-recourse debt financing. These deployments comprehensively prove that Canadian sovereign clusters are not relegated to utilizing trailing-edge or outdated hardware; they are successfully securing tier-one supply chain allocations alongside global competitors.   The Cost Trade-Off: Hardware Economics, Power Tariffs, and the Energy ShockOperating sovereign frontier AI compute involves immense, multi-generational capital expenditure (CapEx) and deeply complex operational expenditure (OpEx). While the federal SCIP funding and private debt markets provide the initial CapEx necessary to secure hardware, the OpEx of Canadian infrastructure is undergoing a radical, state-driven transformation in 2026.The Quebec Hydro Rate Shock and the Erosion of ArbitrageHistorically, Canada's primary competitive advantage in global datacenter hosting was rooted in the province of Quebec's abundant, exceptionally low-cost, and sustainable hydroelectric power. However, the explosive, exponential growth of artificial intelligence has fundamentally altered grid dynamics. Global AI power capacity escalated to approximately 30 GW by late 2025, and Hydro-Québec internal projections indicate that datacenter electricity demand within the province will surge an astonishing sevenfold by 2035, exceeding 1,000 MW.   To prevent these colossal fixed infrastructure and grid expansion costs from being passed onto regular residential consumers—and to politically and economically extract the "full value" of the province's green energy premium—Hydro-Québec formally petitioned the provincial energy regulator (Régie de l'énergie) to double the electricity rates for new data centers exceeding 5 MW of capacity. Set to take effect in the second half of 2026, the new rate establishes a strict baseline of 13 cents per kilowatt-hour (kWh) for large AI data centers, while cryptographic and blockchain operations face an even steeper, punitive hike to an average of 19.5 cents/kWh.   This 13¢/kWh rate fundamentally reshapes the cross-border cost comparison. While it remains nominally lower than the United States national commercial average of 14.12¢/kWh, it severely erodes the historic energy arbitrage advantage against prime US datacenter hubs. For instance, Virginia—the densest and most mature datacenter market in the world—maintains average commercial electricity rates of approximately 11.44¢ to 13.5¢/kWh depending on specific load tiering, while Texas operates at highly competitive commercial rates near 10.7¢/kWh. By achieving cost-parity with the absolute core of the United States infrastructure market, Quebec's sovereign compute clusters can no longer compete strictly on lower operating margins. They must now justify their premium pricing through enhanced privacy, rigid security protocols, and ironclad jurisdictional guarantees.   Comparative Electricity Costs: Sovereign vs. Hyperscale HubsThe impact of grid economics can be quantified by comparing the prevailing rates in major geographic hosting zones. The table below illustrates the narrowing gap between Canadian and US power pricing.JurisdictionSector ClassRate (cents/kWh)Strategic Impact on AI WorkloadsQuebec (H2 2026)Large Data Center (>5 MW)13.00¢Eliminates historical arbitrage; forces competition on sovereignty value.Texas (May 2026)Commercial10.77¢Highly attractive for hyperscale builds due to deregulated energy market.Virginia (May 2026)Commercial11.44¢Intense cluster density maintains competitive rates despite massive demand.US National AvgCommercial14.12¢Represents the baseline operating cost for broadly distributed hyperscale zones.Quebec (Crypto)Blockchain/Crypto19.50¢Punitive rate designed to force out low-economic-benefit workloads.Export to SheetsFrontier Hardware: The Economics of the NVIDIA Blackwell B200Beyond foundational power constraints, the silicon hardware itself dictates the ultimate cost-quality equation. In 2026, the NVIDIA B200 Blackwell architecture has become the undisputed, indispensable standard for frontier AI workloads, completely eclipsing the previous generation's H100 Hopper chips. The B200 represents a monumental leap in hardware engineering, featuring 192 GB of HBM3e memory operating at an unprecedented 8 TB/s bandwidth, along with dedicated support for ultra-low precision FP4 and FP6 calculations, which the older H100 architecture lacks.   This colossal memory bandwidth is critical for the economics of LLM inference. For a standard 70-billion parameter model operating in FP16 precision, an H100 GPU can sustain an inference rate of approximately 24 tokens per second. In stark contrast, the B200 delivers roughly 57 tokens per second—a nearly 140% massive increase in raw throughput.   Consequently, while the B200 hardware commands a significant premium rental price—averaging $4.50 to $6.00 per hour for on-demand instances across global cloud providers, and soaring up to $9.86/hr depending on the cluster size and commitment duration—the ultimate cost-per-token generated actually plummets due to its vast efficiency gains.   ProviderHardware ProfilePricing ModelHourly Rate (per GPU)Market AvailabilityCoreWeaveNVIDIA B200Reserved$2.65Waitlisted / EnterpriseRunPodNVIDIA B200On-Demand$5.98ImmediateInworld ComputeNVIDIA B200On-DemandFrom $6.00Available NowLambdaNVIDIA B200 (16-node)On-Demand$9.862-week to 1-year termsIndustry AvgNVIDIA H100On-Demand$1.38 - $11.01Broad availabilityExport to SheetsFor the Canadian sovereign ecosystem, securing B200 availability is absolutely paramount for remaining technologically relevant. Global supply remains highly constrained, with an estimated 3.6 million units heavily backordered through mid-2026. Sovereign providers face profoundly higher up-front capital risks compared to hyper-capitalized global hyperscalers who have the financial leverage to purchase in massive, multi-billion dollar bulk allocations.   The AI Compute Access Fund and Federal Procurement ConstraintsTo alleviate the financial burden of these staggering hardware costs for domestic innovators, the Government of Canada deployed the AI Compute Access Fund. Managed under the Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, this $300 million fund is specifically designed to tackle the high cost of compute resources and the limited availability of domestic capacity. The fund operates as a direct market intervention, aggressively subsidizing compute costs for eligible Canadian Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs). The structure covers an impressive two-thirds of the eligible costs for utilizing Canadian, cloud-based AI compute services, while only covering half of the costs if the SME opts for a non-Canadian, foreign-hosted compute service.   This two-tiered subsidy explicitly drives commercial demand toward domestic sovereign clusters, artificially insulating local firms from the higher localized capital and energy costs. However, there is a critical, often overlooked constraint in this funding architecture: the eligibility criteria are exceptionally rigid. To access the fund, an entity must be a Canadian-registered for-profit company with fewer than 500 full-time employees, must possess a Canada-based R&D team, and must be actively revenue-generating or possess minimum Series A financing.   Therefore, federal government departments and agencies themselves are entirely ineligible for these specific SME subsidies. Consequently, federal IT procurement under Shared Services Canada must bear the unvarnished, true market rates of sovereign infrastructure. This reality makes the raw, un-subsidized cost delta between a Canadian-hosted B200 instance operating on 13¢/kWh power and a deeply optimized Azure or AWS B200 instance an acute, inescapable fiscal consideration for government planners attempting to balance tight departmental budgets.The Quality Gap: Infrastructure Provisioning versus Ecosystem MaturityIf the cost equation hinges heavily on power tariffs and hardware economics, the quality equation is defined almost entirely by the maturity of the software stack, the breadth of platform services, and developer velocity. Here, the trade-off between emerging domestic sovereign compute and entrenched US hyperscalers is most pronounced and challenging.The Hyperscaler Platform AdvantageHyperscalers do not merely rent out bare-metal GPUs attached to a network connection; they provide highly integrated, frictionless, and secure ecosystems of Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) tools. Amazon Web Services (AWS) offers Amazon Bedrock, a flagship, fully managed serverless service that grants developers immediate API access to a multitude of foundation models—including Anthropic's Claude, Meta's Llama, and Cohere—without requiring the user to provision or manage the underlying GPU hardware.   Microsoft Azure holds a massive structural advantage through its exclusive, multi-billion-dollar partnership with OpenAI, integrating cutting-edge generative AI directly into the ubiquitous Microsoft 365 ecosystem, allowing federal employees to leverage Copilot functionalities seamlessly. Google Cloud Platform (GCP) leverages its Vertex AI platform and deep integration with proprietary silicon (Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs) to power the Gemini model family, earning recognition as a Leader in the 2026 Forrester Wave for Sovereign Cloud Platforms.   These hyperscale ecosystems dramatically reduce the time-to-deployment for complex AI applications. Federal departments utilizing Azure or AWS benefit from automated container orchestration, continuous zero-trust security monitoring via specialized tools like Wiz's Security Graph, and seamless, massively scalable data pipelining. The genuine "quality" of a hyperscaler is rooted not just in the silicon, but in this unparalleled developer velocity, operational reliability, and the elimination of maintenance friction.   The Sovereign Software Challenge and the National Service LayerConversely, pure sovereign infrastructure projects, particularly in their nascent stages of deployment, face a significant risk of functioning merely as high-performance compute (HPC) collocation facilities that severely lack high-level orchestration software. A raw, unmanaged cluster of 1,000 advanced B200 GPUs is exceptionally powerful in theory, but without a mature software ecosystem, federal data scientists must expend vast, highly expensive engineering resources managing container orchestration, load balancing across nodes, model weight distribution, and vital security patching entirely manually.The Government of Canada has astutely recognized this severe vulnerability in its procurement strategy. The SCIP procurement process explicitly bifurcates funding, dedicating immense resources to the "National Service Layer" specifically to prevent the newly minted supercomputer from becoming an unusable, bare-metal silo. The winning National Service Layer provider is strictly mandated to develop the UI/UX portals, extensive APIs, comprehensive AI and machine learning compute-as-a-service frameworks, active data management repositories, and training support necessary to make the hardware accessible.   Furthermore, private sovereign initiatives are actively forging strategic partnerships to rapidly build this vital software stack. For example, Datavault AI has deeply integrated with CyberCatch to overlay continuous-compliance monitoring and automated cybersecurity defenses natively across domestic AI edge platforms. This creates a sovereign software ecosystem designed specifically to rival hyperscaler tooling in heavily regulated industries. Additionally, Microsoft's strategic integration of Cohere's made-in-Canada sovereign models into the Azure Foundry lineup demonstrates a pragmatic hybrid approach, where highly protected domestic intellectual property is deployed efficiently via global cloud platforms.   Ultimately, the quality trade-off in 2026 undeniably favors the hyperscalers for immediate, out-of-the-box application development and rapid deployment. However, the heavily funded sovereign National Service Layer is actively closing this capability gap, aiming to provide customized, highly secure, and functionally comparable environments optimized specifically for foundational model training and sensitive government inference tasks.Synthesizing the Trilemma for Federal Workloads in 2026The complex decision matrix for deploying federal government AI workloads in 2026 cannot be solved by a uniform, monolithic policy directive. The cost, quality, and jurisdiction variables intersect entirely differently depending on the specific data classification and risk profile of the workload in question. Federal IT strategists, operating under the guidance of Shared Services Canada, must rigorously evaluate the landscape across two primary operational vectors: the Protected B paradigm and the Protected C paradigm.The Protected B Paradigm: Leveraging Cryptographic SovereigntyProtected B data encompasses highly sensitive personal information, medical records, detailed financial data, and performance evaluation reports. The unauthorized disclosure or compromise of this data could reasonably be expected to cause serious organizational or individual injury, severe distress, financial loss, or significant damage to reputation. While highly sensitive, it generally does not rise to the existential level of national security or defense intelligence.   For Protected B AI workloads, the optimal trade-off in 2026 heavily favors leveraging the mature, localized hyperscaler ecosystems.Jurisdiction: Hyperscalers have successfully and demonstrably met the Government of Canada's stringent technical requirements for this tier. Google Cloud's Data Boundary for Canada and its successful, rigorous Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS) assessment for Protected B High Value Asset (PBHVA) workloads ensure that technical support is managed exclusively by cleared Canadian personnel and that the data physically resides locally within the nation's borders. Microsoft's aggressive deployment of the Sovereign AI Landing Zone (SAIL) and deep integration of Confidential Compute provides mathematical, cryptographic assurances that data remains completely inaccessible to foreign operators even during active, in-memory processing.   Quality: Utilizing Azure, AWS, or GCP allows federal departments to instantly leverage cutting-edge generative AI models, robust automated cybersecurity integrations—thereby radically reducing the "toxic cloud trilogy" of dangerous misconfigurations and identity exposures—and mature orchestration tools without suffering the severe latency of waiting for the domestic National Service Layer to fully mature and stabilize.   Cost: Hyperscalers absorb the massive, multi-billion-dollar capital risk of hardware procurement, supply chain logistics, and rapid technological refresh cycles. Federal departments benefit directly from highly optimized OpEx models and fiercely competitive on-demand B200 pricing, avoiding the punishing, un-subsidized localized capital costs and the 13¢/kWh energy premium that plague standalone sovereign builds.The theoretical risk of CLOUD Act exposure for Protected B data is deemed an acceptable operational risk by many framework analysts when comprehensively counterbalanced by robust cryptographic controls (like customer-managed HSMs), strictly codified contractual legal defiance clauses, and the unparalleled operational efficiency of hyperscale ecosystems.   The Protected C Paradigm: The Absolute Sovereign MandateProtected C data involves critical national security intelligence, advanced defense logistics, classified architectural schematics, and highly sensitive diplomatic communications. In this domain, the cost-quality-jurisdiction calculus inverses entirely.   For Protected C AI workloads, the optimal—and legally requisite—trade-off demands absolute reliance on true domestic sovereign infrastructure.Jurisdiction: The fundamental, classified nature of Protected C strictly prohibits the acceptance of any theoretical extraterritorial vulnerability. Cryptographic sovereignty is deemed entirely insufficient because the physical hardware, the underlying hypervisor, and the ultimate operational control remain vested in a foreign corporate entity subject to the "possession, custody, or control" doctrine of the United States CLOUD Act. As established by international precedent and explicit French Senate testimony, global hyperscalers cannot unconditionally guarantee absolute immunity from covert or unprecedented US law enforcement mandates. Therefore, Protected C workloads must reside on strictly air-gapped infrastructure that is owned, operated, and legally bound exclusively by Canadian entities. This is precisely the infrastructure envisioned by the SCIP program and bespoke, highly secured deployments by domestic telecommunications giants like TELUS.   Quality: While pure sovereign clusters may currently lack the expansive, user-friendly PaaS maturity of AWS Bedrock or Azure OpenAI, raw computational power is no longer a deficit. Domestic facilities like Alpha Compute are actively and successfully deploying 8 TB/s bandwidth Blackwell B200 architectures concurrently with major US deployments. Furthermore, for highly classified environments, federal data science teams typically build custom, heavily containerized environments regardless of the host, rendering the absence of commercial PaaS tools less detrimental. The forthcoming, heavily funded National Service Layer will further standardize and secure these sensitive workflows.   Cost: In the realm of Protected C data, the fiscal efficiency of the cloud is entirely subordinated to the imperatives of national security. The federal government must willingly absorb the substantial premium of sovereign CapEx and the operational friction of Quebec's elevated 13¢/kWh energy rates. This financial premium is the unavoidable, necessary price of absolute jurisdictional control and national digital independence.   ConclusionThe 2026 landscape of Canadian federal AI compute is defined by the profound tension between the immense gravitational pull of United States hyperscale efficiency and the indispensable, non-negotiable necessity of sovereign jurisdictional control. The Government of Canada has astutely recognized that neither path is universally superior, nor can a single architecture satisfy the vast, disparate needs of the modern federal apparatus.The $2 billion Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, underscored by the massive SCIP infrastructure build and the targeted SME support of the AI Compute Access Fund, successfully ensures that Canada will not be entirely dependent on foreign infrastructure for its most critical operations or the cultivation of its domestic innovation ecosystem. Simultaneously, the aggressive, localized investments by Microsoft, AWS, and Google—manifested in sophisticated sovereign landing zones, localized personnel constraints, and confidential computing enclaves—provide a highly secure, vastly efficient, and cryptographically sound pathway for the vast majority of standard government modernization efforts.   Ultimately, the resolution to the cost-quality-jurisdiction trilemma is a strategically bifurcated deployment architecture. For the immense volume of operational and Protected B federal workloads, utilizing localized hyperscaler architectures maximizes computational quality and operational velocity while maintaining robust, cryptographically enforced data residency. However, for Protected C datasets and classified national security intelligence, the uncompromising requirement for legal absolute sovereignty dictates exclusive reliance on Canada's emerging, Blackwell-equipped domestic sovereign compute clusters, accepting the structural and energy costs as a necessary investment in enduring national resilience and digital self-determination. {\\\"plan_id\\\": \\\"plan-2026-05-11T00:00:00Z-scaleai-tradeoff\\\", \\\"version\\\": 3, \\\"title\\\": \\\"SCALE-AI vs Hyperscalers Trade-off\\\", \\\"steps\\\": [{\\\"id\\\": \\\"step-1\\\",
